skill.name: nature-writing
skill.description: Draft, restructure, or plan Nature-style manuscript sections from author-provided claims, results, figures, notes, or Chinese drafts. Use when the user wants to write or rebuild an abstract, introduction, related-work, method, experiments, discussion, conclusion, title, or full manuscript argument rather than only polish finished prose. Also trigger on general academic-writing requests even without the word "Nature", such as writing a paper from scratch, drafting a manuscript/section, structuring a paper, and Chinese phrasings like 学术写作、科研写作、论文写作、写论文、写paper、SCI写作、帮我写论文、搭论文框架、起草论文、写引言/摘要/讨论.
skill.summary: Draft and structure Nature-style scientific manuscript sections from author notes, results, or Chinese drafts.
skill.author: Community contribution, refactored into static/dynamic layers

security_audit.summary: The static scanner flagged 776 potential issues across 66 files, but manual review confirms nearly all findings are false positives. The skill is an academic writing assistant that drafts manuscript sections. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' hits refer to academic vocabulary like 'hash', 'seed', and 'digest' used in writing contexts. The 'path traversal' hits are YAML fragment-path references in the manifest. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' hits are markdown inline-code formatting throughout prose and examples. The only real filesystem behavior is reading skill fragment files. No prompt injection attempts, no exfiltration, and no malicious intent were detected.

low_findings.title: Fragment-file path references in manifest
low_findings.description: manifest.yaml references paths like ../_shared/core/reader-workflow.md using parent-directory traversal syntax. These are static fragment paths the skill router reads, not user-controlled paths, and represent no injection risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown inline-code formatting flagged as shell backticks
low_findings.description: Extensive use of markdown inline-code backticks throughout prose and examples was misclassified as Ruby/shell execution. All occurrences are formatting tokens for terms like `section`, `paper_type`, `language`, and journal names used as examples in writing guidance.

low_findings.title: Academic vocabulary matched as cryptographic keywords
low_findings.description: Terms such as 'hash', 'seed', 'digest', and 'key' used in scientific-paper writing examples triggered 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections. These are ordinary English words in academic contexts, not code.

low_findings.title: External URLs to public writing resources
low_findings.description: README.md links to two public resources (a Notion page and a GitHub repo) used as attribution for the skill's writing methodology. These are read-only references and do not fetch data into the skill.

content.user_title: Draft Nature-style manuscript sections from your notes
content.value_statement: Researchers often have raw claims, figures, or Chinese drafts but struggle to produce well-structured Nature-style prose. This skill loads the right writing fragments and drafts rigorous, publication-ready manuscript sections.
actual_capabilities: Drafts Nature-style abstract, introduction, method, experiments, discussion, conclusion, and title sections
Plans the one-sentence argument and argument chain before writing prose
Loads paper-type-specific playbooks for research, methods, hypothesis, algorithmic, and review papers
Supports both English notes and Chinese-source notes with zh-to-en translation intent
Adjusts framing for Nature, Nature Communications, or generic scientific journals
Self-audits drafts against an adversarial reviewer checklist for rejection risks
limitations: Does not polish sentence-level English; for that, use a dedicated polishing skill
Cannot invent data, statistics, mechanisms, or novelty that the author has not provided
Does not perform literature search or generate citations automatically
Cannot replace domain expertise; final claims must be verified by the author

use_cases.title: Draft a full manuscript from lab notes
use_cases.description: A graduate student has figures, results tables, and Chinese notes. The skill detects axes, loads matching fragments, and drafts all required sections in Nature style.
use_cases.target_user: Graduate students writing their first Nature submission

use_cases.title: Rebuild the introduction for resubmission
use_cases.description: Reviewers asked for a sharper contribution framing. The skill rewrites the introduction using a challenge-decomposition pipeline and a Nature summary-paragraph funnel.
use_cases.target_user: Postdocs revising a manuscript after reviewer comments

use_cases.title: Generate a methods section from a technical outline
use_cases.description: An engineer has a pipeline diagram and bullet points. The skill organizes them into a module-by-module methods section with motivation, design, and advantages.
use_cases.target_user: ML and engineering researchers writing methods-heavy papers

prompt_templates.title: Draft an abstract from claims and results
prompt_templates.prompt:
I have the following claims and key results from my experiments. Draft a Nature-style abstract following the background-gap-method-result-significance-boundary structure.

Claims: [paste 3-5 sentences]
Results: [paste key numbers, figures, or ablations]
Journal target: Nature Communications

prompt_templates.title: Restructure an introduction using a challenge funnel
prompt_templates.prompt:
My current introduction reads like a literature review. Restructure it using the challenge-decomposition pipeline: field-scale gap, bottleneck of existing work, unresolved problem, our contribution. Here is the draft:

[paste introduction]
prompt_templates.scenario: Revising an introduction after reviewer feedback

prompt_templates.title: Write a methods section from a module outline
prompt_templates.prompt:
Generate a Nature-style methods section from this pipeline outline. For each module explain motivation, design, forward flow, and technical advantages.

Modules: [list modules]
Figures referenced: [list figure numbers]
prompt_templates.scenario: Methods section drafting for an algorithmic paper

prompt_templates.title: Run a rejection-risk self-review
prompt_templates.prompt:
Audit my manuscript against an adversarial reviewer checklist. Identify weak claims, unsupported mechanisms, missing ablations, and ambiguous boundary statements.

[paste full draft or specific section]

output_examples.input: Claims: Our method reduces inference latency by 40 percent on edge devices. Results: 40 percent latency reduction, 2 percent accuracy drop, evaluated on three benchmarks.
output: Detected axes: paper_type=research, section=abstract, language=en, journal=nat-comms
Background: Real-time inference on edge devices is constrained by memory bandwidth and model size.
Gap: Existing compression methods either sacrifice accuracy or require retraining.
Method: We propose a hardware-aware pruning strategy guided by latency profiling.
Key result: On three benchmarks, our method achieves a 40 percent reduction in inference latency with only a 2 percent drop in accuracy, without retraining.
Significance: This enables deployment of competitive models on resource-constrained hardware.
Boundary: Results are validated on classification tasks; generalization to generative tasks remains future work.

output_examples.input: Draft introduction from this draft: Existing work focuses on cloud-based inference. Edge deployment is limited by compute and memory. We propose a lightweight architecture.
output: Funnel opening: Edge deployment of deep models is increasingly critical for latency-sensitive applications, yet device compute and memory budgets remain severe bottlenecks.
Existing attempts: Prior work explores pruning, quantization, and architecture search, but each trades accuracy for efficiency or requires costly retraining.
Unresolved gap: A deployable solution that preserves accuracy without retraining remains missing.
This work: We introduce a lightweight architecture that achieves competitive accuracy with a fraction of the compute budget, deployable on commodity edge hardware.

best_practices: State the detected axes in one short line before drafting so the user can correct cheaply
Write the one-sentence argument first, then build sections around it
Load only the fragments the axes select, never the full static layer
List missing evidence under 'Assumptions or missing inputs' instead of inventing content
Run the adversarial self-review before considering a draft complete
anti_patterns: Do not invent data, mechanisms, statistics, sample sizes, or novelty claims
Do not load every fragment in static/ into context for a single request
Do not write a Chinese-source draft by mirroring the Chinese sentence order into English
Do not skip the planning steps even when the user asks for prose immediately

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and a polishing skill?
faq.answer: This skill drafts and structures manuscript sections from raw notes. A polishing skill improves sentence-level English in an already-finished draft.

faq.question: Can this skill write in Chinese?
faq.answer: The skill translates the intent and argument from Chinese notes into English prose. It does not produce Chinese output.

faq.question: Which journals does the skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Nature, Nature Communications, and a generic scientific-journal mode. Subjournals of Nature are mapped to the nature mode.

faq.question: Does the skill fabricate data or citations?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly avoids inventing data, mechanisms, statistics, sample sizes, or novelty claims. Missing evidence is listed as an assumption.

faq.question: How does the skill decide which fragments to load?
faq.answer: The router reads manifest.yaml, detects the axis values from the user request, and loads only the matching fragment files. The static layer is never fully loaded into context.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for a non-Nature journal?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the generic journal mode for standard scientific journals. For Nature-family journals including subjournals, use the nature mode.
